clean_story:
Vibrations with power. Energy you can hear.

The phrase sound as energy comes from physics: sound is motion—air molecules shoved in waves, like a crowd doing "the wave" in a stadium but invisible. Each push carries force. A whisper nudges air gently; a jet engine slams it like a hammer.

Here’s the unseen labor: When a tuning fork hums, its prongs bend 440 times per second, shoving air into ripples that travel to your eardrum. That drum vibrates too, turning pressure back into signals your brain calls "A4 note." No air? No sound. Astronauts on moon walks radioed home because vacuum has nothing to shove—silence isn’t absence, but a lack of pusher.

Harness it: Ultrasound machines fire 20,000 vibrations per second into bodies. When waves hit a fetus’s bones, they bounce back like echoes in a cave, drawing images from time delays. Or take "acoustic levitation"—scientists trap water droplets midair using sound waves so precise they cancel gravity’s pull, holding liquids like invisible tweezers.

Sound isn’t just noise. It’s measurable work.
Three related subjects are resonance disasters, animal sonar hunting, and music as medicine.
